planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall to allow natural cold stratification over winter; seed pressed lightly onto prepared soil surface will germinate the following spring.

directions: Sow seed in fall directly into a prepared garden bed or naturalized area, pressing seed gently into the soil surface without covering deeply. Cold winter temperatures provide the stratification needed to break dormancy, and seedlings will emerge in early spring. Plants grow slowly in their first year as they establish a deep taproot, so avoid disturbing the planting site. Once established, Heart-leaved Golden Alexander is long-lived and will self-sow modestly in favorable spots.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into moist, humus-rich soil; cold stratification over winter dramatically improves germination rates the following spring.

directions: Scatter seed onto moist, humus-rich soil in a partially shaded site in early fall, barely covering with a thin layer of fine soil or leaf mold. Natural winter cold breaks seed dormancy; do not attempt to rush germination with artificial heat. Plants require consistent moisture and will decline in dry or compacted soils, so amend planting beds with compost before sowing. Expect slow establishment over the first two seasons before plants begin reliable late-summer bloom.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall into well-drained woodland soil; cold winter temperatures fulfill stratification requirements for spring germination.

directions: Sow seed in early fall into a woodland garden bed with well-drained, slightly acidic soil, pressing seed to the surface and covering with only a dusting of soil or leaf litter. Cold stratification through winter is essential for breaking dormancy, and seedlings should emerge by mid-spring. This rare southeastern gentian is sensitive to waterlogged roots, so ensure drainage is adequate even in moist woodland conditions. Plants typically require two or more growing seasons before producing their striking fall-blooming, purple-striped flowers.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seed in fall into rich, well-drained woodland soil; cold stratification over winter is required for germination, and plants will spend many years as a rosette before ever flowering.

directions: Sow fresh seed in fall into deep, humus-rich woodland soil at a site that will not be disturbed for many years, as plants spend a decade or more building a large basal rosette before sending up their dramatic flowering stalk. Lightly cover seed and allow winter cold to provide necessary stratification; germination is slow and uneven, so patience is essential. Select the planting location carefully, since American Columbo develops a deep root system and resents transplanting at any stage. After the plant finally flowers and sets seed, it will die, so allow seed to mature and scatter for future generations.

planting.note: Start seed indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost; surface sow onto moist seed-starting mix and do not cover, as light aids germination.

directions: Surface sow seed onto moist, fine-textured seed-starting mix indoors 6-8 weeks before last frost, pressing seed gently onto the surface without covering since light is required for germination. Keep the medium consistently wet, as Brook Lobelia naturally grows along stream banks and in calcareous fens where moisture is never limiting. Transplant seedlings to a sunny, consistently moist garden bed or boggy site after the last frost date has passed, spacing plants 6-12 inches apart. Established plants spread slowly and can be divided in early spring to propagate and fill in wet areas.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall into moist, rich forest soil in a shaded location; cold stratification over winter improves germination, and seedlings emerge in spring.

directions: Sow seed in fall into deeply amended, humus-rich soil beneath deciduous trees where the site remains consistently moist but not waterlogged. Cover seed lightly with sifted leaf mold and allow the winter chill to stratify the seed naturally before spring germination. Stoneroot grows large with age and benefits from generous spacing so that its broad, lemon-scented leaves can spread without crowding neighboring plants. Once established, it is a long-lived woodland fixture that needs little care beyond maintaining soil moisture and a mulch of decomposing leaves.
